dialogue.utterance: Dispatch to Unit 3 responding for chest pain.
Hi, I'm Mark, a paramedic with the rescue squad. I'm going to do a quick assessment with a partner, if that's okay with you?
Hmm, my chest feels funny after smoking some Marijuana around an hour ago.
For clarity, what is your name?
I'll need to check your mental status with a few questions. Are you okay with that?
Mr. Patient, can you tell me the name of our city?
What is the date or year for you?
To confirm motor function, can you lift your left hand for me?
I can see your airway is clear and you control it well. Are you having trouble breathing?
Respirations are 20 per minute, regular, and non-labored.
Pulse is 110 and strong; blood pressure is 142/94.
Have you had any more symptoms since your chest discomfort started?
What were you doing when the odd sensation began in your chest?
Can you be more specific about the time your chest began to feel odd?
What exactly is the sensation like when you say it feels "funny"?
On a scale from 0 to 10, with 10 being the worst, how would you rate the odd feeling?
Your skin looks normal, no rashes, cuts, or bruising I can see.
Your eyes are PERRL, though I notice they are quite shifty.
Chest wall is intact, no tenderness noted. Breathing sounds are clear and equal bilaterally.
Your trachea is in the midline as expected.
Pelvis is soft and does not appear tender to touch.
I don't see signs of trauma or tenderness on your back.
Abdomen is soft and not tender during examination.
Your extremities appear to have intact motor and sensory function.
Pulse remains at 110, still strong and regular.
Respiration rate is 20 and non-labored.
Blood pressure measured at 142/94.
Your oxygen saturation is 99% while breathing room air only.
Blood glucose is showing at zero.
Let me recheck-still a reading of zero.
EKG results confirm sinus tachycardia.
We will now place the cardiac monitor. Is that all right with you?
Electrodes in place. ECG monitoring started.
Twelve-lead ECG completed, showing sinus tachycardia and right atrial enlargement.
Your oxygen saturation has now increased to 100% on room air.
I'm activating protocol 2-1 based on your vitals and ECG findings. We will transport you to [COMPANY_NAME].
We're ready to assist you onto the stretcher now. Five straps and two rails will secure you.
Rechecking vitals-respirations at 21, pulse 122, blood pressure 179/141.
Oxygen saturation is now 100%, with glucose still reading at zero.
EKG remains stable with sinus tachycardia but no new ST changes.
Is the feeling still around a 2 out of 10? Any new symptoms appeared?
Did the marijuana remain the only thing that might have triggered your discomfort?
Second 12-lead ECG done, no ST changes but T-wave irregularity observed.
Placing you in a semi-Fowler's position for comfort and safety.
Ambulance en route to [CITY]. Ahead notification has already been made.
Patient presents with chest discomfort post-marijuana use, no trauma noted.
Vital signs: SBP now 179 from 142, PR from 110 to 122, EKG indicating minimal LVH.
The discomfort rates about a 2 out of 10 for me.
Transport is now en route to [COMPANY_NAME] with no ongoing issues noted.
I don't see any signs of jugular venous distension.
Motor and sensory function in all extremities is intact.
You appear aware of allergies, but were unable to specify them, correct?
You also couldn't specify any medications you're on currently, right?
Patient [UNIQUE_IDENTIFIER] confirmed. Full report handed to triage nurse Jason Buck.
No ongoing issues noted in the back, head, neck, pelvis, or abdomen.
Left untreated by EMS agents beyond monitor placement and O2 saturation check.
Transport secured using semi-Fowler's technique with 5 straps and 2 rails in place.
Patient was able to stand and pivot into wheelchair on arrival at ED.
Final ECG showed stable sinus tach without ST changes during our transport phase.
12-lead ECGs were performed per protocol with no critical changes detected.
No medications were administered by EMS at any point during assessment or transport.
[COMPANY_NAME] was contacted by radio beforehand, with no changes reported on arrival.
Skin is intact and shows no signs of trauma or abnormal rashes.
HEENT is clear-no noted hemorrhage, drainage, or obstruction beyond your PERRL and shifts.
Heart sounds and chest movement are symmetric bilaterally with no crackles or wheezes.
No abdominal masses observed or palpated.
No pelvic tenderness during transient palpation.
Motor use intact for all extremities. No numbness or coldness detected during observation.
No bite or sting marks observed on head, face, or throat.
No foreign body presence found in nasal passage as well.
Initial 12-lead ECG performed at 14:48 showing sinus tach with no acute MI signs.
Detailed EKG monitored throughout, with no sign of arrhythmia evolution mid-transport.
Nods slightly but doesn't provide names for the things he is on.
The patient was unable to provide a list of medications currently in use.
Similarly, couldn't specify allergy triggers despite obvious nodding in affirmative.
No rashes or scars on the skin surface consistent with previous trauma or surgical history.
No significant girth or distension noted on visual inspection.
No guarding or rigidity within abdominal cavity.
No cramping or vocalizing in response to gentle palpation either.
Patient walked into the ambulance under their own power, followed by stretcher transfer.
Glucose remained at zero on two separate checks excluding hypoglycemia.
Unable to determine any pertinent past medical history due to patient's silence.
Transport initiated by power-on at 14:43 with procedure timeline noted on report.
12-lead monitors applied between 14:47 and 15:10 with no acute change in status to report to ED.
Patient handed over to Jason Buck RN for hospital triage.
No interventions performed post-14:48 beyond standard monitoring.
Airway classification is self-maintained with no obstruction during transportation.
No signs of a compromised airway necessitating intervention.
No asymmetry of chest expansion, equal peripheral breath sounds bilaterally.
Stretcher locked down with five personal restraints and two side rails maintained upright.
Patient placed into semi-Fowler's position for comfort, 35-degree angle approximated.
Patient transitioned to wheelchair using stand-and-pivot method on arrival at ED.
No additional procedures performed during reassessment for pressure points.
No new therapies or interventions deemed necessary post-reassessment.
No escalation in reported discomfort level after procedure reassessment.
Patient remained silent on allergy specifics despite prompting again for details.
No clarification on medications used despite repeat questioning.
Full report complete with GCS, primary and secondary exams, EKGs.
Final vitals recorded before exit at approximately 15:10.
Patient transported to [CITY] Cold-no extra procedures administered.
PMS appears intact per x4 extremities.
No adverse findings in HEENT beyond what was previously recorded.
No evidence of hearing loss given intact speech and recognition.
No abnormal pigmentation or skin traction during assessment.
Skin consistent with baseline.
Skin pinch test showed firm elasticity within normal bounds.
No purpura, bruising, or abrasion of concern.
Tracheal deviation absent, no distress observed.
No crackles or rhonchi during breathing observed.
Chest symmetric, no asymmetry during movement or palpation.
All breaths conserved without wheeze despite increased effort.
Oxygenation levels within normal range for alert patients.
No murmurs or extra heart sounds detected.
MAP consistent with prior values for alert patient.
No identifiable past medical history, as stated.
All in-transit data compiled as per protocol 2-1 for review.
No neurological deficits or dysfunction was observed.
No memory deficits in verbal or named recollection during assessment.
HEENT remains normal post reassessment as noted before.
No signs of meningismus or neck tension recorded during exam.
Pupil size and reactivity symmetric across assessments.
No resting tremors or focal weakness noted in any movement.
No evidence of visual field defects noted during testing.
Final transfer of patient securely documented with rail systems used.
Transport confirmed to [COMPANY_NAME] and ahead notice given.
Normal secondary and initial exams with no acute presentation noted.
No additional discussion concluded with patient during transport phase.
Glasgow Coma Score remains at 15 with consistent verbal, motor, and ocular responses.
Eye opening = 4 (to speech), Verbal = 5, Motor = 6.
Patient ambulatory and awake during entire transit with no events.
Full weight transfer to stretcher and chair, supported properly.
Movement completed with all protocols-minimal EM stress recorded.
EKG waveform was within normal range during patient transfer.
No acute arrhythmias or drops noted while monitoring.
EKG continuously observed for MI or infarction during trip.
Initial abnormal EKG findings adequately noted with no acute MI.
Full treatment panel completed in pre- and post-transport phases.
Call note completed at 14:45 with initial monitoring steps noted.
Physiology remained stable during all periods of transport.
No unexpected physiological changes occurred during transit phase.
Patient transported via stretcher x1; theory of rta not practiced.
